title: Integrated Information Security Analysis Team Handoffs
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the NIST 800-53 programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with NIST 800-53 control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST 800-53 IR-10 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 IR-10 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.51: Integrated Information Security Analysis Team Handoffs.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.51' running on snow:incident with assignment_group transitions, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Integrated Information Security Analysis Team Handoffs' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper NIST 800-53 control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the NIST 800-53 control objective.

dataSources: `snow:incident` with assignment_group transitions — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident category="Security" earliest=-30d
| transaction number maxspan=24h startswith="assignment_group=SOC" endswith="assignment_group=CTI"
| where duration>3600
| table number, duration, assignment_group, short_description
description: Tracks cross-team comments and assignments between SOC and CTI for IR-10 integrated infosec analysis team collaboration.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:incident. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Validate `transaction` performance — limit indexes; (2) alternatively use `streamstats` on sorted events; (3) KPI for handoff latency; (4) monthly leadership review. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:incident`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised security assessment activities** — Planned security control assessments, continuous monitoring scans, and Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) validation tests generate expected control deviation signals that are part of the Assessment & Authorisation process.

2. **System authorisation boundary changes** — During ATO re-authorisation, system boundary expansions, or inheritance model updates, temporary control gaps may appear in the monitoring baseline until the updated security plan is approved by the Authorising Official.

3. **Interconnection Security Agreement (ISA) activity** — Data exchanges with authorised external systems operating under signed ISAs produce cross-boundary traffic that triggers network monitoring alerts but is covered by the formal interconnection agreement.

4. **FIPS 199 impact-level re-categorisation** — When system categorisation changes (e.g., Moderate to High), the transition period produces control baseline mismatches until the enhanced controls are fully implemented per the approved implementation schedule.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nist_800_53_exception_filter` KV Store lookup containing approved assessment schedule entries, POA&M IDs, ISA identifiers, and system re-categorisation project IDs. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches with valid exception entries.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `IR-10`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIST 800-53 IR-10 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.51: Integrated Information Security Analysis Team Handoffs." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:incident`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Validate `transaction` performance — limit indexes. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** alternatively use `streamstats` on sorted events. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** KPI for handoff latency. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** monthly leadership review. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Integrated Information Security Analysis Team Handoffs," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIST 800-53 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search uses `| transaction` to group events that share a correlation identifier into a single result row. Transactions are analytically powerful but operationally expensive: Splunk must buffer events in memory until the transaction closes, which caps the practical event volume (default `maxevents=1000` per transaction, `maxopentxn=10000` concurrent transactions).

Scale warning: for compliance searches that must cover multi-day spans with potentially tens of thousands of open transactions, `| transaction` will either truncate or OOM. Prefer `| stats earliest(...) latest(...)` grouped by the same correlation ID — it produces the same analytical result with no memory cap and 5-20x better performance.

When `| transaction` is the right choice: when you need the event sequence preserved (e.g. detecting a specific ordering of events like 'login -> elevate -> access -> logout'), when you need `maxpause` or `maxspan` constraints between events, or when you need `startswith`/`endswith` markers. If you only need min/max of a field across the group, always use `| stats`.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.14.51: Integrated Information Security Analysis Team Handoffs]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.14.51,reg=NIST_800-53
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `assignment_group`, `duration`, `number`, `short_description`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table assignment_group, duration, number, short_description
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `duration`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(duration) as p50 perc90(duration) as p90 perc99(duration) as p99 max(duration) as hi
```

The current threshold `3600` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.14.51: NIST 800-53 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`slow handoffs`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`median duration`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIST 800-53-specific failure modes:**

• Control baseline selection: NIST 800-53 has Low/Moderate/High/Privacy baselines via FIPS 199 categorisation. A detection written to a High baseline threshold produces false alarms against Low-categorised systems. Join with the system categorisation register `nist_800_53_system_categorization.csv` before thresholding.

• CA-7 continuous monitoring cadence: specifies 'ongoing' authorisation activities. Detections supporting CA-7 must produce metrics at the organisation's ConMon cadence (monthly is common for most agencies) — weekly fires are fine but not themselves evidence of monthly compliance.

• Control inheritance: in a cloud-hybrid deployment, some controls are inherited from the Cloud Service Provider. Detections must not flag 'missing' controls the CSP provides (documented in the SSP via Control Inheritance matrix); reference the SSP appendix and suppress matching findings.

• SP 800-53 Rev 5 restructure: controls were renumbered between Rev 4 and Rev 5. Detections that cite Rev 4 numbering (e.g. AC-2.12) may not match the SSP if the organisation has migrated. Store both Rev numbers in the UC metadata and reconcile.
grandmaExplanation: We tracks cross-team comments and assignments between SOC and CTI for IR-10 integrated infosec analysis team collaboration. That helps federal and agency security reviews match what the systems actually do day to day.
